title: Gridbadger: Live Flip Run
description: Guide a rooftop gridbadger across a live neon grid, swiping between tiles to dodge cursor takeovers and cash in risky last-second flips for bigger scores.

howToPlayJson.title: Gridbadger: Live Flip Run
howToPlayJson.objective: Survive a 2-4 minute skyline run by dodging incoming cursor claims, escaping live tile eruptions, and building a score multiplier by crossing hot tiles just before they flip.

controls.input: Swipe up, down, left, or right
controls.outcome: The gridbadger hops to the next rooftop tile in that direction.

controls.input: Stay on a flashing tile for a moment
controls.outcome: You bait a takeover and earn a higher multiplier if you leave before the tile goes live.

controls.input: Swipe through highlighted hot tiles during a takeover wave
controls.outcome: You bank bonus points fast, but a mistimed move gets you caught in the flip.

steps: Start on a small rooftop grid while ghostly live cursors sweep in from the edges and mark tiles they are about to claim.
Each claimed tile flips from safe to flashing to live-electric; standing on a tile when it turns live is an instant fail.
Cursor paths get denser every wave, creating moving walls, cross-cuts, and corner traps that force quick lane changes.
Your combo rises when you step off a flashing tile just before takeover, chaining 'front-run' escapes without getting hit.
Every few seconds a Price Surge marks several tiles gold: crossing them before they flip adds a big multiplier boost, but those tiles flip a
howToPlayJson.scoring: Score increases from survival time, escaped takeovers, and chained front-run bonuses. The multiplier climbs when you leave flashing tiles at the last safe beat, and resets if you touch a live tile or let too much time el
tips: Watch the cursors, not just the hazards; their approach tells you which escape lane will vanish next.
Use the center lanes early, then cut outward when surge tiles appear so you keep multiple exits open.
Don't chase every Price Surge; skipping a bad one is better than losing a full combo.
Audio warning chirps are the safest cue for last-second escapes when the screen gets crowded.
